sections.Abstract: We study the effectiveness of multilingual pre-trained language models (mPLMs) for cross-lingual transfer to low-resource languages. We evaluate mBERT, XLM-R, and mT5 on NER and classification tasks across 12 low-resource languages, finding that lexical overlap between source and target languages is the strongest single predictor of transfer success (r=0.81).
sections.Introduction: Cross-lingual transfer allows models trained on high-resource languages to be applied to low-resource settings. Despite the success of models like mBERT and XLM-R, failure modes remain poorly understood, particularly for morphologically rich languages with limited lexical overlap with English.
sections.Methods: We conduct a systematic evaluation across 12 low-resource languages from 6 language families. Transfer performance is measured using zero-shot and few-shot settings. Language pairs are characterised by lexical overlap (LO), morphological complexity index (MCI), and script similarity (SS).
sections.Results: XLM-R achieves best average F1 (52.3) across tasks. Lexical overlap (r=0.81) and script similarity (r=0.74) are the strongest predictors of transfer success. Few-shot (k=10) examples close 40% of the zero-shot to full-supervision gap. Morphological complexity negatively correlates with transfer (r=-0.63).
sections.Conclusion: Cross-lingual transfer is effective but highly sensitive to source-target language proximity. Morphologically rich languages remain a significant challenge. Future work should investigate language-adaptive fine-tuning and multilingual data augmentation for agglutinative languages.

claims.claim_text: Lexical overlap is the strongest predictor of cross-lingual transfer success (r=0.81).
claims.evidence_text: Pearson correlation between lexical overlap and zero-shot F1 across 12 languages is r=0.81.

claims.claim_text: XLM-R outperforms mBERT and mT5 on average across all 12 low-resource languages.
claims.evidence_text: XLM-R achieves best average F1 (52.3) compared to mBERT (44.1) and mT5 (48.7).

claims.claim_text: 10 few-shot examples close 40% of the zero-shot to full-supervision performance gap.
claims.evidence_text: Few-shot (k=10) examples reduce the gap between zero-shot and full supervision by approximately 40% on average.

gaps.gap_description: Transfer to morphologically rich agglutinative languages remains poor despite strong average results.
gaps.actionable_direction: Language-adaptive tokenization (e.g., morpheme-aware BPE) combined with language-specific fine-tuning heads may significantly improve transfer to agglutinative languages.

gaps.gap_description: The mechanisms by which few-shot examples improve transfer are not analysed or explained.
gaps.actionable_direction: Probing studies and attention visualisation on few-shot vs zero-shot models would clarify whether improvements come from calibration, vocabulary coverage, or structural adaptation.

agentA.hypothesis: Morpheme-segmentation-aware BPE tokenisation pre-trained jointly with XLM-R will improve NER F1 on agglutinative languages by ≥10 points.
agentA.rationale: Current subword tokenisation fragments morpheme boundaries in agglutinative languages, losing structural information. Morpheme-aware tokenisation preserves these boundaries.

agentB.hypothesis: Adding 5,000 synthetically generated silver-standard sentences per target agglutinative language via back-translation will improve transfer F1 by ≥8 points.
agentB.rationale: Data augmentation via back-translation is well-validated in NMT and can be applied cheaply without architecture changes.

agentA.hypothesis: Attention head probing on layer 8-12 of XLM-R will reveal morphological structure heads specifically activated by few-shot examples in agglutinative languages.
agentA.rationale: Probing studies on BERT-family models have successfully identified syntactic structure heads, motivating similar analysis for morphology.

agentB.hypothesis: Measuring KL divergence between zero-shot and few-shot contextual embedding distributions will quantify the representation shift induced by few-shot adaptation.
agentB.rationale: Embedding distribution analysis provides a model-agnostic explanation of improvement, with clear quantitative output.

reflections.original_hypothesis: Morpheme-segmentation-aware BPE tokenisation will improve NER F1 on agglutinative languages by ≥10 points.
reflections.improvement_plan: Add specificity to the training data requirement, clarify the morpheme segmentation tool used (e.g., Morfessor), and add a comparison against standard XLM-R fine-tuning baseline.
reflections.revised_hypothesis: XLM-R fine-tuned with Morfessor-segmented BPE vocabulary (trained on ≥1M tokens per language) will improve NER F1 on Turkish, Finnish, and Hungarian by ≥10 points over standard subword tokenisation, measured on test splits matching the paper's experimental setup.
